QF.id: Clubbing, Set-Off and Carry Forward of Losses_18

question.text: Mrs. Lata gifted Rs.5,00,000 to her minor daughter (age 10, no disability) who earned Rs.35,000 as interest income. Lata's total income is Rs.12,00,000 and her husband's income is Rs.8,00,000. What amount is added to Lata's total income due to clubbing?

answer.text: Rs.35,000 — full amount clubbed with the higher-earning parent
Rs.33,500 — clubbed with husband as he is the father, with Rs.1,500 exemption
Rs.33,500 — clubbed with Lata (higher income parent) after Rs.1,500 per child exemption
Nil — income from gifts by parents is not clubbed

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under Section 64(1A), minor child income is clubbed with the parent having higher total income. Lata's income (Rs.12,00,000) > Husband's income (Rs.8,00,000), so the minor's income is clubbed with Lata. Exemption under Section 10(32) = Rs.1,500 per child. Amount clubbed = Rs.35,000 - Rs.1,500 = Rs.33,500. **The Trap:** Option B correctly computes the exemption but clubs with the wrong parent (father instead of higher-income parent). The rule is gender-neutral — it goes to the parent with higher income, regardless of whether it's the father or mother. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A tests the "higher income parent" rule. X-B adds complexity with disability and skill/talent exceptions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In dual-income families, always identify which parent has higher income for clubbing. If incomes fluctuate year to year, the clubbing parent may change annually.
